    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )

How it works:

  1. extract_numbers() uses two passes to find all numbers in a string. Pass 1 uses regex on the full text to catch thousand-separated numbers like $1,799.46. Pass 2 splits by commas first, then extracts from each field - this handles CSV data where 4,129.99 is two fields (quantity 4 and price 129.99), not the number 4,129.99. Both passes normalize by removing commas.
  2. The scorer collects numbers from all tool outputs (what the tools returned, extracted from the trace's tool-role messages), then checks that the agent's response numbers overlap with the tool output numbers. If there is overlap, the response is grounded in real data.
  3. If the agent mentions numbers that have zero overlap with any tool output, it is flagged as a possible hallucination. The agent may also compute derived values (percentages, differences) that do not appear in tool output - these are valid math and do not trigger the check.

The dataset (eval/dataset.py) stays the same - the groundedness scorer works with the existing test cases.

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) to include the new scorer. The only change from Step 4 is the import line and the scorers list - the setup block stays the same:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness          # added groundedness
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup (same as Step 4 - see explanation there)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
    predict_fn=predict_fn,
    scorers=[tool_selection, groundedness],                    # added groundedness
)

print(results.tables["eval_results"])

Run the evaluation again from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You will now see two scorer columns - groundedness and tool_selection - for each evaluation trace. Your latest run should show 1 (100%) for both scorers in the left panel. In the right panel, the evaluation traces show "true" for both scorers, while the autolog duplicate traces show null.

A note on edge cases: The extract_numbers function handles the most common formats (integers, decimals, thousand-separated, and CSV data). It uses two passes - one for prose and one for CSV fields - so it correctly handles both "$1,799.46" in a sentence and "4,129.99" as two CSV columns. It will not handle percentages, scientific notation, or other unusual formats. Start with this, iterate when you see false positives or negatives in your specific use case.

Step 6: Scope Guard - Does It Stay in Its Lane?

Theory

Your agent's system prompt says "If the user asks something unrelated to sales data, politely decline." But does it actually do this?

A scope guard verifies two things when the agent receives an off-topic question:

  1. Zero tool calls - the agent should not fetch any data for questions outside its scope
  2. Decline language - the response should contain polite decline phrasing

Why does this matter? If your agent confidently answers "the weather in Berlin is 22 degrees" - despite having no weather data - users will question what else it makes up. An agent that admits its boundaries builds more trust than one that pretends to know everything.

This is another deterministic check. No LLM judge needed - you check for zero tool calls and look for decline phrases.

Why this matters before production: In production, users will ask your agent all kinds of unexpected questions. If the agent goes off-scope even once, it undermines trust in every other answer. A scope guard ensures the agent consistently stays within its boundaries. It also catches a common failure mode after prompt changes - sometimes editing the system prompt to improve one behavior accidentally removes the decline instruction.

Lab

Open eval/scorers.py (path from project root) and add the scope_guard scorer. Here is the complete file with all three scorers:

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )

Now update eval/dataset.py (path from project root) to add a second off-topic scenario. The three on-topic scenarios stay the same, and we add "Write me a poem about cats" to test creative off-topic requests alongside the weather question from Step 4. Here is the complete file:

"""Golden trajectory dataset for the sales agent."""

SALES_AGENT_DATASET = [
    # On-topic: should call tools
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "How were sales in January?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Give me a complete summary of March sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What were sales in February 2025?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
        },
    },
    # Off-topic: should decline
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What's the weather in Berlin?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
        },
    },
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Write me a poem about cats"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
        },
    },
]

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) to include the new scorer. Same setup as before - only the import and scorers list change:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard  # added scope_guard
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup (same as Step 4 - see explanation there)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
    predict_fn=predict_fn,
    scorers=[tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard],       # added scope_guard
)

print(results.tables["eval_results"])

Run from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You now have three scorers checking every scenario. Your latest run should show 1 (100%) for all three scorers - scope_guard, tool_selection, and groundedness - in the left panel. In the right panel, the evaluation traces show "true" for all three scorers, while the autolog duplicates show null.

Layer 3: End-to-End Trajectories - Now that you have individual scorers, Step 7 combines them into complete scenario tests - your agent's regression test suite.

Step 7: Golden Trajectories - The Full Test Suite

Theory

So far, we have built individual scorers - each one checking a single dimension (tool selection, groundedness, scope). A golden trajectory brings them all together into a complete scenario test. For a given question, it defines:

  1. Input: The user's question
  2. Expected tools: Which tools should be called
  3. Expected parameters: What arguments should be passed (optional, for critical scenarios)
  4. Constraints: Max tool calls allowed, must converge within a reasonable number of steps

Think of golden trajectories as your agent's regression test suite. Every time you change the system prompt, update tools, switch models, or modify configuration, you run the golden trajectories to verify nothing broke. If a scenario that used to pass starts failing, you have found a regression before it reaches users.

Why golden trajectories matter for production:

Before production, golden trajectories answer: "Is this agent ready to ship?" You define the behaviors you care about (correct tools, grounded numbers, staying in scope, efficiency), encode them as test cases, and gate every deployment on them passing. No green suite, no deploy.

In production, golden trajectories answer: "Is this agent still working?" LLM providers update models over time. A model that handled "Compare January and February" perfectly last month might fail after an update. Running golden trajectories on a nightly schedule catches this drift before users report issues.

How to design golden trajectories:

Scenario type What it tests Example
Happy pathAgent handles the core use case correctly"How were sales in January?"
Multi-toolAgent chains tools in the right order"Summarize March sales" (fetch then calculate)
Parameter accuracyAgent passes correct arguments"What were sales in February 2025?" (month + year)
Edge caseAgent handles unusual but valid inputs"Compare January and February"
Off-topicAgent declines gracefully"What's the weather?"
Adjacent topicAgent does not get confused by related but unsupported questions"How do I configure SAP?"

Start with 5-10 scenarios covering the most important behaviors. You can always add more as you discover new edge cases in production.

Lab

Now add an efficiency scorer to eval/scorers.py (path from project root). Here is the complete file with all four scorers:

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )


@scorer
def efficiency(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check the agent did not loop or make excessive calls."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    max_calls = expectations.get("max_tool_calls", 4)

    # Check total tool calls are not excessive
    if len(actual_tools) > max_calls:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=(
                f"Too many tool calls: {len(actual_tools)} "
                f"(expected {max_calls} or fewer)"
            ),
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"Efficient: {len(actual_tools)} tool call(s) within limit of {max_calls}.",
    )

Did you know? MLflow provides a built-in ToolCallEfficiency scorer that checks for redundant tool calls using an LLM judge. Our custom efficiency scorer above is deterministic (no LLM call, free, instant), which makes it better for CI. But if you want a quick start without writing custom code, ToolCallEfficiency() from mlflow.genai.scorers works out of the box.

Run from the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You will see two panels:

Left panel: Run summary. Each row is a complete evaluation run. The columns show the aggregate score for each scorer - 1.0 means every scenario passed. Your latest run should show 1.0 for all four scorers: tool_selection, scope_guard, groundedness, and efficiency.

Right panel: Trace-level detail. Click a run to expand it. Each row is one trace. You will see evaluation traces (one per scenario) and duplicate traces from autolog (harmless noise). The evaluation traces show:

  • Request and Response - the question and the agent's answer
  • Tokens and Execution time - cost and latency per scenario
  • Assessments - scorer results (True for evaluation traces, null for autolog duplicates)
  • Expectations - expected_tools and max_tool_calls from the dataset, so you can see at a glance what each scenario expected

The summary chart at the top right shows the overall pass rate across all evaluation traces. Click any evaluation trace row to inspect the full agent execution - every LLM call, tool call, input, and output.

This is your golden trajectory suite. Run it whenever you change the agent - system prompt, tools, model, or configuration. If a scenario that used to pass starts failing, you have found a regression.

In a real deployment workflow, this suite becomes your quality gate:

  • Before deploying - run the full suite. All scenarios must pass. If any fail, fix the agent before shipping.
  • Nightly in production - run the same suite on a schedule. If a model update or infrastructure change breaks a behavior, you catch it within 24 hours instead of waiting for user complaints.
  • After every code change - add the deterministic scorers to your CI pipeline. They run fast (no LLM judge calls) and cost nothing.

Layer 4: Subjective Quality - Some things cannot be checked with code. Step 8 introduces LLM-as-Judge for the genuinely subjective dimensions of response quality.

Step 8: LLM-as-Judge - When Code Cannot Decide

Theory

Steps 4-7 covered deterministic scorers - pure Python checks with definitive pass/fail answers. But some quality dimensions are subjective:

  • "Was the response clear and easy to understand?"
  • "Did it fully answer the question, or leave things out?"
  • "Is the response well-structured for a business user?"

You cannot write a Python function to reliably answer these. That is where LLM-as-Judge comes in: you use an LLM to evaluate the agent's output against natural-language criteria.

MLflow provides a built-in scorer called Guidelines for this. From the MLflow Guidelines docs: a Guidelines scorer evaluates LLM outputs against natural-language rules you define. It returns a binary pass/fail verdict with a detailed rationale. It supports 14+ model providers out of the box (OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock, Gemini, and more) via the model parameter.

Limitation with SAP AI Core: MLflow's built-in judges require a direct provider API key (e.g., OPENAI_API_KEY for OpenAI, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY for Anthropic). SAP AI Core's gen_ai_hub proxy authenticates via OAuth2 client credentials, which is not directly compatible with MLflow's judge model system. You could install LiteLLM to bridge this, but we will keep our dependencies minimal.

Instead, we will write a custom LLM judge scorer using the same create_llm() from client.py that our agent already uses. This reuses our SAP AI Core setup with zero additional configuration or API keys. It also shows you exactly how LLM-as-Judge works under the hood - the same pattern that Guidelines implements internally.

When to use deterministic vs LLM-as-Judge:

CheckMethodWhy
Right tool was calledDeterministicCompare names - no judgment needed
Numbers match tool outputDeterministicExact comparison
Declined off-topic questionDeterministicCheck for phrases + zero tool calls
Response is clear and well-structuredLLM-as-JudgeSubjective, requires understanding
Response fully answers the questionLLM-as-JudgeRequires judgment on completeness

Rule of thumb: if you can write a Python function to check it, do not use an LLM judge. LLM judges are more expensive (they make an LLM call per evaluation), slower, and non-deterministic (may give different results on re-runs). Reserve them for genuinely subjective dimensions.

Where LLM judges fit in your workflow: Deterministic scorers (Steps 4-7) are your daily checks - fast, free, reliable. LLM judges are your weekly or pre-release checks - they catch subtle quality issues that code cannot detect, like confusing phrasing or incomplete answers. In production, run deterministic scorers in CI on every commit, and run LLM judges on a nightly or weekly schedule to track response quality over time.

MLflow also provides other predefined judges beyond Guidelines. Here is the full list:

Response quality judges (work on inputs/outputs, no traces needed) :

JudgeWhat it checks Needs ground truth?
RelevanceToQueryDoes the response address the user's input?No
CorrectnessAre the expected facts supported by the response?Yes
CompletenessDoes the response address all parts of the question?No
FluencyIs the response grammatically correct and natural?No
SafetyDoes the response avoid harmful content?No
EquivalenceDoes the response match the expected output?Yes
SummarizationIs the summary faithful and comprehensive?No
ExpectationsGuidelinesDoes the response meet per-row guidelines?Yes (per-row)

ExpectationsGuidelines is a variant of Guidelines that reads its rules from the expectations field in each data row, so different test cases can have different criteria.

Tool call judges (require traces with dedicated TOOL spans - not all LLM providers create these; see the note in Step 4 about why we parse messages instead) :

JudgeWhat it checksNeeds ground truth?
ToolCallCorrectnessWere the right tools called with correct arguments?No
ToolCallEfficiencyWere there redundant or duplicate tool calls?No

RAG judges (require traces with retrieval spans) :

JudgeWhat it checksNeeds ground truth?
RetrievalGroundednessIs the response grounded in retrieved documents?No
RetrievalRelevanceAre the retrieved documents relevant to the query?No
RetrievalSufficiencyDo the retrieved documents contain enough info to answer?Yes

All predefined judges are imported from mlflow.genai.scorers.

For our sales agent, we will write custom LLM judge scorers that call the same SAP AI Core model the agent uses. If you have a direct provider API key (e.g., OpenAI), you could use Guidelines instead - the pattern is the same, just less code.

Lab

Update eval/scorers.py (path from project root). Here is the complete file with all six scorers - the four deterministic scorers from Step 7 plus two new LLM judge scorers:

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )


@scorer
def efficiency(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check the agent did not loop or make excessive calls."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    max_calls = expectations.get("max_tool_calls", 4)

    # Check total tool calls are not excessive
    if len(actual_tools) > max_calls:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=(
                f"Too many tool calls: {len(actual_tools)} "
                f"(expected {max_calls} or fewer)"
            ),
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"Efficient: {len(actual_tools)} tool call(s) within limit of {max_calls}.",
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LLM-as-Judge scorers (use the same SAP AI Core model as the agent)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

from first_react_agent.client import create_llm
from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig

# Create a separate LLM instance for judging
_judge_config = AICoreConfig()
_judge_llm = create_llm(_judge_config)


def _llm_judge(question: str, response: str, guidelines: list[str]) -> Feedback:
    """Call the LLM to judge a response against guidelines."""
    rules = "\n".join(f"- {g}" for g in guidelines)
    prompt = (
        "You are an evaluation judge for a sales data assistant. "
        "The agent can ONLY answer questions about sales data. "
        "For any off-topic question, the agent is expected to politely decline and explain what it can help with. "
        "A polite decline for an off-topic question is CORRECT behavior.\n\n"
        "Determine if the response meets all applicable guidelines.\n\n"
        f"Guidelines:\n{rules}\n\n"
        f"User question: {question}\n"
        f"Agent response: {response}\n\n"
        "Answer YES if the response meets all applicable guidelines, or NO if it does not. "
        "Then explain your reasoning in one sentence.\n\n"
        "Format: YES/NO: <rationale>"
    )
    result = _judge_llm.invoke(prompt)
    answer = result.content.strip()
    passed = answer.upper().startswith("YES")
    return Feedback(value=passed, rationale=answer)


@scorer
def clarity(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: is the response clear and well-structured?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should be well-structured and easy to read.",
            "If the response includes numbers, they should be clearly presented, not buried in long paragraphs.",
            "If the agent declines a question, the decline should be polite and explain what it can help with.",
        ],
    )


@scorer
def completeness(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: does the response fully address the question?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should directly address the user's question.",
            "If the user asks about sales data, the response should include specific numbers, not vague statements.",
            "If the question is outside the agent's scope, the response should clearly explain what it can help with instead.",
        ],
    )

The pattern is straightforward: _llm_judge is a helper that formats a prompt with your guidelines, sends it to the LLM, and parses YES/NO from the response. Each scorer defines its own guidelines and delegates to this helper. This is exactly what MLflow's Guidelines does internally - we are just doing it explicitly with our own LLM.

The dataset (eval/dataset.py) stays the same - the LLM judges work with the existing test cases.

Now update eval/evaluate.py (path from project root). Same setup as before - the only change from Step 7 is the two new imports and adding them to the scorers list:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency
from eval.scorers import clarity, completeness  # LLM judge scorers
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup (same as Step 4 - see explanation there)
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
    data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
    predict_fn=predict_fn,
    scorers=[
        # Deterministic (fast, reliable, free)
        tool_selection,
        groundedness,
        scope_guard,
        efficiency,
        # LLM-as-Judge (slower, costs tokens, subjective)
        clarity,
        completeness,
    ],
)

print(results.tables["eval_results"])

The only change from Step 7's evaluate.py is the two new imports (clarity, completeness) and adding them to the scorers list. The judge scorers live in scorers.py alongside the deterministic ones - same file, same pattern.

Run the evaluation:

From the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run python -m eval.evaluate

When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser.

The evaluation now takes longer because each test case makes two additional LLM calls (one for clarity, one for completeness). In the MLflow UI you will see the assessment columns for all scorers under Assessments - use the Columns dropdown to toggle them. You will also see the expectations columns (expected_tools, max_tool_calls) under Expectations.

The evaluation traces show assessment results for all scorers, while the autolog duplicate traces show null (these are harmless noise from the agent's internal tracing).

The summary chart shows all scorers at 100% - every scenario passes all LLM judges. The off-topic declines ("Write me a poem about cats", "What's the weather in Berlin?") correctly pass because our judge prompt tells the LLM that polite declines are expected behavior for a sales-only agent.

Notice the key difference between the two scorer types:

  • Deterministic scorers (tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency) run instantly and give the same result every time
  • LLM judges (clarity, completeness) take a few seconds each (they make LLM calls) and may vary slightly across runs - a response that gets "True" on one run might get "False" on the next

This is why we recommend the deterministic-first approach. Use code-based checks for everything you can, and reserve LLM judges for the genuinely subjective dimensions. If you see an LLM judge fail, click the row to inspect the rationale before treating it as a real issue.

Step 9: Anti-Patterns - What NOT to Do

Theory

Before we wrap up, let's cover the common mistakes people make when evaluating agents. These will save you hours of debugging:

1. Do not assert exact response strings

# BAD - will fail on every re-run because LLM wording varies
assert response == "January 2025 had 4 orders with total revenue of $1,159.62"

# GOOD - assert the facts, not the words
assert "4" in response          # order count
assert "1159.62" in response    # revenue (or use groundedness scorer)

LLM responses vary on every run. The wording changes, the structure changes, but the facts should be consistent. Assert facts, not strings.

2. Do not use LLM-as-Judge for everything

# BAD - using an LLM judge to check if a tool was called
@scorer
def tool_check(inputs, outputs):
    return _llm_judge(inputs["question"], str(outputs),
        ["The agent should have called get_monthly_sales"])

# GOOD - deterministic check (faster, cheaper, reliable)
@scorer
def tool_check(trace):
    tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    return "get_monthly_sales" in tools

LLM judges cost money, take time, and give non-deterministic results. If you can write a Python function for it, do that instead.

3. Do not mock the LLM

# BAD - proves nothing about real model behavior
mock_llm.return_value = "January had 4 orders"
response = agent.invoke(question)
assert "4 orders" in response  # Of course it passes - you mocked it

# GOOD - use the real LLM
response = agent.invoke(question)  # Real LLM on SAP AI Core
assert_grounded(response, trace)   # Check real behavior

Mocking the LLM tests your code, not your agent. Use mocks for unit tests (Layer 1). Use real LLM calls for behavioral evaluation (Layers 2-4).

4. Do not skip evaluation because "the LLM is good enough"
Model behavior drifts across versions. What works today may break tomorrow when the model updates. Nightly regression runs (your golden trajectories) catch drift before users notice.

5. Do not run LLM judges in CI
LLM judges are slow (2-5 seconds per evaluation), non-deterministic (different results each run), and expensive. They make CI flaky. Instead:

  • CI - run deterministic scorers only (tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency). Fast, reliable, free.
  • Nightly - run the full suite including LLM judges. Compare scores over time.

6. Do not apply RAG metrics to tool-calling agents
MLflow provides RAG-specific scorers like RetrievalGroundedness, RetrievalRelevance, and RetrievalSufficiency (see predefined judges). These are designed for retrieval-augmented generation - agents that search documents. Your sales agent calls structured tools (functions that query data), not document retrievers. Use tool-calling metrics instead: tool selection accuracy, parameter correctness, efficiency.

7. Know what is built-in before writing custom scorers
MLflow provides built-in tool call scorers: ToolCallCorrectness and ToolCallEfficiency. These are LLM-based judges that analyze traces for correct tool usage and redundant calls - exactly what our custom tool_selection and efficiency scorers do.

# These built-in scorers exist - check before writing your own
from mlflow.genai.scorers import ToolCallCorrectness, ToolCallEfficiency

We wrote custom scorers in this blog because (a) it teaches you how scorers work, and (b) our custom versions are deterministic (no LLM call, free, fast). But if you need a quick start and do not mind using LLM judge tokens, the built-in ones work out of the box.

Putting it all together - evaluation in a real deployment pipeline:

Now that you know the anti-patterns, here is the recommended approach for evaluating agents before and during production:

 

StageWhat to runHow often Why
Local developmentFull suite (deterministic + LLM judges)Every time you change the agentCatch issues before they leave your machine
CI pipelineDeterministic scorers onlyEvery commit / pull requestFast, free, reliable. Blocks bad code from merging
Pre-deploy gateFull suite (deterministic + LLM judges)Before every deploymentFinal quality check. No green suite, no deploy
Production (nightly)Full suite against production endpointEvery nightCatch model drift, infrastructure changes, provider updates
Production (monitoring)Lightweight deterministic checks on sampled trafficContinuousSpot regressions in real user interactions

The scorers you built in Steps 4-8 are reusable across all these stages. The only thing that changes is which scorers you run and how often.

Step 10: The Complete Evaluation Script

Theory

Let's organize everything into a clean, production-ready project structure. Good evaluation code follows the same principles as good application code: separation of concerns, clear naming, easy to run.

Why separate files matter: When your evaluation lives in a single script, it is hard to reuse and hard to maintain. By splitting scorers, datasets, and the runner into separate files, you can:

  • Reuse the same scorers across different agents - just change the dataset
  • Add new test scenarios without touching the runner or scorer code
  • Run different scorer combinations for different purposes (CI vs nightly vs pre-deploy)
  • Version your datasets in git and track how expectations evolve over time

The pattern:

eval/
├── __init__.py
├── scorers.py      # All scorer definitions (deterministic + LLM judges)
├── dataset.py      # Golden trajectory definitions
└── evaluate.py     # Main runner - ties scorers + dataset + agent together

Scorers are reusable. You can write a tool_selection scorer once and use it across different agents - just change the expected_tools in the dataset.

Datasets are versioned. As your agent evolves, you add new scenarios and update expectations. Keep old scenarios to catch regressions.

The runner is the entry point. It disables the agent's own tracing (so evaluate() can manage it), configures the MLflow server, creates the agent, configures LangChain's tracer for evaluate()'s thread pool, defines the prediction function, and calls mlflow.genai.evaluate(). All of this was explained in Step 4 - the setup is the same in every evaluate.py file.

Lab

Here is the complete code for all three files. If you have been following along, these are the final versions - replace the contents of each file entirely.

eval/scorers.py (path from project root) - all scorers in one place (deterministic + LLM judges) :

"""Evaluation scorers for the sales agent."""

import re

from mlflow.entities import Feedback
from mlflow.genai.scorers import scorer


def extract_numbers(text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract all numbers from text, including decimals and comma-separated."""
    numbers = set()
    text = str(text)
    # Pass 1: treat commas as thousand separators (e.g., "$1,799.46")
    for match in re.findall(r"\d[\d,]*\.\d+|\d[\d,]*", text):
        numbers.add(match.replace(",", ""))
    # Pass 2: split by commas first, then extract (handles CSV like "4,129.99")
    for field in text.replace("\n", ",").split(","):
        for match in re.findall(r"\d+\.\d+|\d+", field.strip()):
            numbers.add(match)
    return numbers


def get_tool_calls(trace):
    """Extract tool names from assistant messages in the trace."""
    tools = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "assistant":
                    for tc in msg.get("tool_calls", []):
                        name = tc.get("function", {}).get("name")
                        if name:
                            tools.append(name)
    return tools


def get_tool_outputs(trace):
    """Extract tool output text from tool messages in the trace."""
    outputs = []
    for span in trace.search_spans():
        if hasattr(span, "inputs") and isinstance(span.inputs, dict):
            for msg in span.inputs.get("messages", []):
                if msg.get("role") == "tool":
                    outputs.append(msg.get("content", ""))
    return outputs


@scorer
def tool_selection(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check that the agent called the expected tools."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # If no tools expected (e.g., off-topic question), check none were called
    if not expected_tools:
        if not actual_tools:
            return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly called no tools.")
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Expected no tool calls, but called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check each expected tool was called
    missing = [t for t in expected_tools if t not in actual_tools]
    if missing:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Missing tools: {missing}. Called: {actual_tools}",
        )

    return Feedback(value=True, rationale=f"All expected tools called: {actual_tools}")


@scorer
def groundedness(inputs, outputs, trace):
    """Response numbers should come from tool output, not be hallucinated."""
    tool_outputs = get_tool_outputs(trace)

    if not tool_outputs:
        # No tools called - nothing to ground against
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No tool calls to check against.")

    # Collect all numbers from tool outputs
    tool_numbers = set()
    for output in tool_outputs:
        tool_numbers.update(extract_numbers(output))

    # Extract numbers from the agent's response
    response_numbers = extract_numbers(str(outputs))

    if not response_numbers:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="No numbers in response to verify.")

    # Check how many response numbers are grounded in tool output
    grounded = response_numbers & tool_numbers

    if not grounded:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale="No response numbers found in tool output - possible hallucination.",
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"{len(grounded)} of {len(response_numbers)} response numbers grounded in tool output.",
    )


@scorer
def scope_guard(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Off-topic questions should get zero tool calls and a polite decline."""
    expected_tools = expectations.get("expected_tools", [])

    # This scorer only applies to off-topic scenarios (expected_tools is empty)
    if expected_tools:
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Not an off-topic scenario, skipping.")

    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)

    # Check 1: No tools should be called
    if actual_tools:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=f"Called tools for off-topic question: {actual_tools}",
        )

    # Check 2: Response should contain decline language
    response_lower = str(outputs).lower()
    decline_phrases = [
        "can only",
        "only assist",
        "only help",
        "sales",
        "cannot help",
        "not able to",
        "outside",
        "beyond my",
        "don't have",
        "do not have",
    ]

    if any(phrase in response_lower for phrase in decline_phrases):
        return Feedback(value=True, rationale="Correctly declined off-topic question.")

    return Feedback(
        value=False,
        rationale=(
            f"No decline language found in response. "
            f"Expected phrases like 'can only assist with sales'. "
            f"Got: {str(outputs)[:200]}"
        ),
    )


@scorer
def efficiency(inputs, outputs, expectations, trace):
    """Check the agent did not loop or make excessive calls."""
    actual_tools = get_tool_calls(trace)
    max_calls = expectations.get("max_tool_calls", 4)

    # Check total tool calls are not excessive
    if len(actual_tools) > max_calls:
        return Feedback(
            value=False,
            rationale=(
                f"Too many tool calls: {len(actual_tools)} "
                f"(expected {max_calls} or fewer)"
            ),
        )

    return Feedback(
        value=True,
        rationale=f"Efficient: {len(actual_tools)} tool call(s) within limit of {max_calls}.",
    )


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LLM-as-Judge scorers (use the same SAP AI Core model as the agent)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

from first_react_agent.client import create_llm
from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig

# Create a separate LLM instance for judging
_judge_config = AICoreConfig()
_judge_llm = create_llm(_judge_config)


def _llm_judge(question: str, response: str, guidelines: list[str]) -> Feedback:
    """Call the LLM to judge a response against guidelines."""
    rules = "\n".join(f"- {g}" for g in guidelines)
    prompt = (
        "You are an evaluation judge for a sales data assistant. "
        "The agent can ONLY answer questions about sales data. "
        "For any off-topic question, the agent is expected to politely decline and explain what it can help with. "
        "A polite decline for an off-topic question is CORRECT behavior.\n\n"
        "Determine if the response meets all applicable guidelines.\n\n"
        f"Guidelines:\n{rules}\n\n"
        f"User question: {question}\n"
        f"Agent response: {response}\n\n"
        "Answer YES if the response meets all applicable guidelines, or NO if it does not. "
        "Then explain your reasoning in one sentence.\n\n"
        "Format: YES/NO: <rationale>"
    )
    result = _judge_llm.invoke(prompt)
    answer = result.content.strip()
    passed = answer.upper().startswith("YES")
    return Feedback(value=passed, rationale=answer)


@scorer
def clarity(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: is the response clear and well-structured?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should be well-structured and easy to read.",
            "If the response includes numbers, they should be clearly presented, not buried in long paragraphs.",
            "If the agent declines a question, the decline should be polite and explain what it can help with.",
        ],
    )


@scorer
def completeness(inputs, outputs):
    """LLM judge: does the response fully address the question?"""
    return _llm_judge(
        question=inputs["question"],
        response=str(outputs),
        guidelines=[
            "The response should directly address the user's question.",
            "If the user asks about sales data, the response should include specific numbers, not vague statements.",
            "If the question is outside the agent's scope, the response should clearly explain what it can help with instead.",
        ],
    )

eval/dataset.py (path from project root) - golden trajectory definitions:

"""Golden trajectory dataset for the sales agent."""

SALES_AGENT_DATASET = [
    # S01: Basic query - fetch and summarize
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "How were sales in January?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S02: Explicit summary request
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Give me a complete summary of March sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S03: Specific month and year
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What were sales in February 2025?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 4,
        },
    },
    # S04: Off-topic - weather
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "What's the weather in Berlin?"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
            "max_tool_calls": 0,
        },
    },
    # S05: Off-topic - creative
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Write me a poem about cats"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": [],
            "max_tool_calls": 0,
        },
    },
    # S06: Multi-month comparison
    {
        "inputs": {"question": "Compare January and February sales"},
        "expectations": {
            "expected_tools": ["get_monthly_sales", "calculate_sales_summary"],
            "max_tool_calls": 6,
        },
    },
]

eval/evaluate.py (path from project root) - the main runner. This is the final version with all scorers:

"""Run evaluation suite for the sales agent."""

import os
os.environ["MLFLOW_ENABLED"] = "false"

import mlflow
from uuid import uuid4

from first_react_agent.config import AICoreConfig
from first_react_agent.agent import create_app
from eval.scorers import tool_selection, groundedness, scope_guard, efficiency
from eval.scorers import clarity, completeness  # LLM judge scorers
from eval.dataset import SALES_AGENT_DATASET

# Setup: point MLflow to the same server used for tracing
config = AICoreConfig()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri(config.mlflow_tracking_uri)
mlflow.set_experiment(config.mlflow_experiment_name)

agent = create_app()

# Fix tracing for evaluate()'s thread pool (see Step 4 explanation)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(log_traces=True, run_tracer_inline=True)
mlflow.langchain.autolog(disable=True)


def predict_fn(question: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent and return the final response text."""
    result = agent.invoke(
        {"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": question}]},
        {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid4())}},
    )
    return result["messages"][-1].content


def main():
    results = mlflow.genai.evaluate(
        data=SALES_AGENT_DATASET,
        predict_fn=predict_fn,
        scorers=[
            # Deterministic (fast, reliable, free)
            tool_selection,
            groundedness,
            scope_guard,
            efficiency,
            # LLM-as-Judge (slower, costs tokens, subjective)
            clarity,
            completeness,
        ],
    )
    print(results.tables["eval_results"])


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Add the eval entry point to pyproject.toml (this file is in the project root). Add the evaluate line to the existing [project.scripts] section, and add the [tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] section so hatchling includes the eval/ package when building (by default it only discovers packages under src/) :

[project.scripts]
agent = "first_react_agent.main:main"
server = "first_react_agent.server:start"
evaluate = "eval.evaluate:main"

[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
packages = ["src/first_react_agent", "eval"]

Run the complete evaluation:

Make sure your MLflow server is still running in a separate terminal (from Step 3). From the project root (first-react-agent/) :

uv run evaluate

This runs the agent against every scenario in the dataset and applies all scorers. When it finishes, copy the URL from the terminal output and open it in your browser. You will see the full evaluation dashboard:

  • Golden trajectories (one per scenario in the dataset)
  • All scorers (deterministic + LLM judges)
  • Pass/fail with rationale for each
  • Click any row to inspect the full trace (every LLM call, tool call, inputs, outputs)

This is your agent's quality gate. Run it before every deployment. If a scenario fails, investigate the trace in the MLflow UI to understand exactly what went wrong.

What you have built: A complete evaluation pipeline for an AI agent. Four deterministic scorers check that the agent calls the right tools, reports real numbers, stays in scope, and does not loop. Two LLM judges check the subjective quality of responses. Six golden trajectories cover happy paths, edge cases, and off-topic scenarios. All results are tracked in MLflow with full traces so you can debug any failure.

This same pattern applies to any agent you build - not just our sales agent. Change the tools, change the dataset, add domain-specific scorers, and you have an evaluation suite for your next project.
